questions.question: You are flying over Lake Winnebago on a 010° course at 89 mph TAS, and the winds aloft are 040° at 19 kts. If the lake is 25 nm long, how far are you from the northern shore when the time to continue equals the time to return?
How far is your Equal Time Point from LAX? (Assume the wind shifts at the ETP.) [[TABLE]]
questions.context: Use the following information for questions 15-18. The distance between LAX and HNL is 2,084.1 NM on a true course of 252°. Cruise altitude is 32,000', ISA+9°C, airspeed of 462 KTAS.

questions.question: How long will it take you from LAX to reach your ETP?
questions.context: Use the following information for questions 15-18. The distance between LAX and HNL is 2,084.1 NM on a true course of 252°. Cruise altitude is 32,000', ISA+9°C, airspeed of 462 KTAS.

questions.question: You are on a 348 km flight on a 223° true course, and you are flying at 173 mph TAS in winds aloft of 190° at 23 kts. How long into the flight will the time to continue equal the time to return?

The flight from Oshkosh, WI to Columbus, OH is 339 NM on a true course of 132°. With a cruise TAS of 129 knots and winds aloft at 7,500' MSL of 085° at 24 knots, how far along in the flight would the time to return to Oshkosh equal the time remaining to Columbus?

choices.text: Halfway into the 3 hour 3 minute flight

questions.question: Referencing the previous question, the eastern shore of Lake Michigan is 202 NM from Columbus. Where would the equal time point be in relation to the shore?

choices.text: Prior to crossing over the shore

questions.context: Referenced question 18: The flight from Oshkosh, WI to Columbus, OH is 339 NM on a true course of 132°. With a cruise TAS of 129 knots and winds aloft at 7,500' MSL of 085° at 24 knots, how far along in the flight would the time to return to Oshkosh equal the time remaining to Columbus?

questions.question: Referencing the previous two questions, what is the groundspeed if the pilot decided to turn back to Oshkosh?
questions.context: Referenced question 18: The flight from Oshkosh, WI to Columbus, OH is 339 NM on a true course of 132°. With a cruise TAS of 129 knots and winds aloft at 7,500' MSL of 085° at 24 knots, how far along in the flight would the time to return to Oshkosh equal the time remaining to Columbus? [[DIVIDER]] Referenced question 19: Referencing the previous question, the eastern shore of Lake Michigan is 202 NM from Columbus. Where would the equal time point be in relation to the shore?

questions.question: You are planning a flight at 6500' with a true course of 049° at 137 KTAS. Winds aloft are 290@33 kts. If the flight is 314 km, how long after departure will the time to continue equal the time to return?

You are planning a 276 km cross country flight at 138 KTAS. You will be flying over remote terrain, so how long into the flight will the time to continue equal the time to return? Your true course is 227° and the winds aloft are 190° at 27 knots.

Crossing a lake that is 37 sm from shore to shore, you wish to know your equal time point. Your current course is 309° at a ground speed of 213 km/h. The winds aloft are 355° at 15 knots. How far across the lake will you be when the time to continue equals the time it would take to turn back?

You are flying to Oshkosh and debating flying over Lake Michigan, or going around. The narrowest portion is 56 NM along a 286° course cruising at 106 KTAS. If the winds are 325° at 15 kts how far after leaving the eastern shore would it be an equal amount of time to continue or turn around?

You are 59 NM into your 153 NM flight when your passenger starts having chest pains. Knowing it will take you 3 minutes to complete the calculations, would it be faster to turn back to your origin or continue to the destination? You are cruising at 8,500' (ISA) and 117 KIAS. The winds aloft at your altitude are 290° at 22 KTS and you are flying a true course of 131°.

choices.text: It is a little over 2 minutes faster to continue to the destination.
It is about 2 and a half minutes faster to turn back.
It is just under 4 minutes faster to continue to the destination.
It is about 5 and a half minutes faster to turn back.

questions.correctChoice: It is a little over 2 minutes faster to continue to the destination.

questions.question: Cruising from KEYW to MYNN (092° TC, 483 NM away) your ETP to your first overwater alternate is 50 NM from KEYW. From that point you know the course to your alternate of 07FA is 046°. Cruising at 185 KTAS with winds at 284° at 32 KTS, how far from 07FA is your equal time point?

You are traveling between Oakland and San Francisco course of 229° and distance of 9.6 NM. The current wind is 310° at 15 knots and you are cruising at 93 KTAS. At what point is your time to San Francisco the same as returning to Oakland?

Just after takeoff, you notice your oil temperature getting slightly high. Since it is still in the green range, you decide to continue, but you want to make sure you know your options. Your trip is 97 nm on a 114 true course, with winds aloft forecast to be 340@38 kts. Your planned cruise is 128 KTAS, burning 9.8 GPH. How far from your departure airport will it be faster to continue to the destination than to turn back?

Using the information from the previous question, and knowing your course across the lake will be 31 nautical miles, how far can you fly out over the lake before the time to continue equals the time to turn back?
questions.context: Referenced question 25: You've decided to fulfill a longtime dream and fly into this year's AirVenture in Oshkosh, Wisconsin. You have faith in your single engine airplane, and have decided to fly across Lake Michigan rather than around it. Your course across the lake will be 285°, and the winds at your chosen altitude of 8,500 feet are 220° at 27 knots. Indicated airspeed is 105 knots. The temperature and pressure at 8,500 are both standard. Before you go any farther, if something goes wrong, how cold will the water below be in Fahrenheit if latest reports indicate a water temperature of 17°C?

questions.question: The winds are 150°@20 kts and you are flying a course 220° to get from ZYX to GKC (185 nm) at 120 KTAS. How far from ZYX is your equal time point?
How long will it take to reach the equal time point?
questions.context: Referenced question 6: The winds are 150°@20 kts and you are flying a course 220° to get from ZYX to GKC (185 nm) at 120 KTAS. How far from ZYX is your equal time point?

questions.question: You depart your home airport on a course of 265° for a destination 96 NM away. If you are planning to fly 105 KTAS and the winds are 240° @ 23 KTS, how long into the flight until it will take as long to reach your destination as it would to turn around and fly home?
How far from your destination will you be at this time?
questions.context: Referenced question 9: You depart your home airport on a course of 265° for a destination 96 NM away. If you are planning to fly 105 KTAS and the winds are 240° @ 23 KTS, how long into the flight until it will take as long to reach your destination as it would to turn around and fly home?

questions.question: With 28 gallons of usable fuel, beyond what point along the flight will it be faster to continue to KTYS rather than return to KMRN? For this question use cruise ground speed as the average for the whole flight.
questions.context: Use the following information for questions 11-15: Joey plans a flight from KMRN (1270') to KTYS (979') at 8,500' MSL on a true course of 277°. The total distance is 118 NM and he will cruise at 145 MPH TAS and 8.7 GPH with winds aloft 240° at 14 KTS. His average climb performance will be 650 FPM at 92 KTAS and 9.8 GPH with average winds of 215° at 15 KTS. His average descent performance will be 900 FPM at 110 KTAS and 5.2 GPH with average winds of 320° at 8 KTS.

questions.question: If you have calculated your equal time point to be 158 NM after departure, what ground speed will you maintain outbound if the total distance to fly is 540 KM and your planned ground speed should you need to return is 165 MPH?
On the way back, 52 sm from GRB, your friend is starting to experience chest tightness. At this point would it be quicker to continue to GRB, or return to TVC?

choices.text: Continue to GRB, you passed your equal time point 13 nm ago
Return to TVC, you're short of your equal time point by 3 nm
Return to TVC, you're short of your equal time point by 5 nm
Return to TVC, you're short of your equal time point by 7 nm

questions.correctChoice: Return to TVC, you're short of your equal time point by 3 nm
questions.context: Use the following info for questions 30-32: [[NEXT_LINE]] Dist. = 110 nm Magnetic Course (GRB-TVC) = 083 TAS = 121 mph [[NEXT_LINE]] Magnetic Winds = 310@35 knots Fuel Flow = 5.5 gph [[NEXT_LINE]] Assume the same performance for climb and descent.

questions.question: You wish to fly across Lake Michigan, but decide it would be a good idea to determine your equal time point across the lake in the unfortunate event that something goes wrong. If the wind is forecast to be out of 230° at 23 knots, the distance across the lake is 73 nautical miles with a true course of 270°, and your TAS is 120 knots, at what distance from your departure point will the time to continue to your destination be equal to the time it takes to return to your starting point?

You are planning to fly from Milwaukee Timmerman (KMWC) due east to Muskegon, MI (KMKG) in your C172 at 115 ktas. The weather for the trip looks great with clear skies and forecast winds 310@27 kts to help you along. Because the majority of the 79 nm flight will be over water you decide to determine when your time to continue will be equal to the time to return home. This point will be how far from KMKG?
From that point how long would it take you to return to Milwaukee?
questions.context: Referenced question 31: You are planning to fly from Milwaukee Timmerman (KMWC) due east to Muskegon, MI (KMKG) in your C172 at 115 ktas. The weather for the trip looks great with clear skies and forecast winds 310@27 kts to help you along. Because the majority of the 79 nm flight will be over water you decide to determine when your time to continue will be equal to the time to return home. This point will be how far from KMKG?

questions.question: You decide to live on the edge and fly a Cessna 172 from Palm Beach International, Florida to Grand Bahama International, Bahamas. Using the following information, how far from Grand Bahama will you be when it will take just as long to continue to Grand Bahama as it will to turn around and go back to Palm Beach? [[NEXT_LINE]] Distance between airports: 76 nm [[NEXT_LINE]] Pressure Altitude: 7,000' MSL [[NEXT_LINE]] Indicated Airspeed: 116 KIAS [[NEXT_LINE]] OAT: 6°C [[NEXT_LINE]] True Course from Palm Beach: 095° [[NEXT_LINE]] True Wind: 302° at 39 mph [[NEXT_LINE]] Fuel On Board/Fuel Flow: 48 US gallons / 63 lb./hr.

You are looking for a new death defying experience so you take a 172 and decide to fly across the largest lake in the United States, Lake Superior. You will be flying across the length of it at 350 sm. You will be cruising at 107 kts with a wind at 200@22. Your true course is 265°. How far away from the west shore is the point at which it will take you the same amount of time to return back the way you came as it does to continue to the other side of the lake?
At what time in your trip across Lake Superior will you be at your equal time point?
questions.context: Referenced question 4: You are looking for a new death defying experience so you take a 172 and decide to fly across the largest lake in the United States, Lake Superior. You will be flying across the length of it at 350 sm. You will be cruising at 107 kts with a wind at 200@22. Your true course is 265°. How far away from the west shore is the point at which it will take you the same amount of time to return back the way you came as it does to continue to the other side of the lake?

questions.question: Johnny departs from an airport on a true course of 157° while the winds at his altitude, 7500' MSL, are blowing from 220° at 24 knots. With 212 nautical miles total distance to travel, how many miles after departing will the time it takes to continue to his destination be equal to the time it takes to return to his departure airport? The outside air temperature is ISA+12°C and his indicated airspeed is 153 mph. Altimeter is 29.92" Hg. Fuel on board 2 hours 30 minutes.

You are planning a flight from airport ABC to airport XYZ, 177 NM away. True course is 253°. Winds are 290@17 knots with an OAT of 5°C above standard. If your TAS will be 115 knots, how far from XYZ will you be when the time to continue equals the time to return?

You are enroute to your grass strip at a cruising altitude of 12,500 ft. where the OAT is -20°C. CAS is 127 knots and the local altimeter setting is 29.42. Fuel on board is 33 gal burning at a rate of 12 gph. Winds are 290@17 knots. Just then you look down and notice the KDAY airport below you. You know KDAY is 110 NM from your grass strip. Assuming you hold a TC of 310°, how far from KDAY will you be when the time to continue to the grass strip equals the time to turn back and fly to KDAY?
